results.possibleIdentity: Jim Rigg is a Kansas-based artist and graphic designer who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is a graduate of Wichita State University (WSU) with a Fine Arts degree in Graphics. Rigg built a career that frequently involved collaborations between artistic and technical disciplines, fitting the mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. He worked as a creative professional in broadcast design for approximately 20 years at KSN, a television station in Wichita, where he partnered with technical staff such as broadcast engineer Don Hogg. Rigg later served as the Design Director at Kansas State University (K-State) in Manhattan, Kansas, before his retirement. His artistic output includes monochromatic drawings of historic aircraft, such as the Travel Air Type R Racing Monoplane (circa 1980), and watercolors like "Dance of the Tulips." His potential association with E.A.T. is supported by the significant E.A.T. presence in Wichita during the 1960s and 1970s, driven by figures like Boeing art director William Allan Fetter, who was an active E.A.T. member and pioneer of computer graphics.

results.possibleIdentity: John Harris (born 1937) is an American artist recognized for his involvement in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for his collaborative works with Fred Stern (born 1936), specifically the installation titled 'K.E.P.-1 Self' (1968). Harris was an active member of E.A.T., a non-profit organization founded to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. His work was featured in significant E.A.T. projects and exhibitions, including 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum (1968–1969) and the Pepsi Pavilion at the 1970 World's Fair (Expo '70) in Osaka, Japan. He also participated in the 1968 competition sponsored by E.A.T. and the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA). His practice often intersected with electronic and kinetic media, reflecting the technological focus of the E.A.T. circle.

results.possibleIdentity: Gloria Sher (born circa 1928) was a prominent American theatrical producer and media collaborator active from the late 1960s through the 1970s and beyond. She is most notably recognized for winning a Tony Award for Best Musical in 1975 as a producer of "Shenandoah." During the late 1960s, she was involved in the experimental and underground film scene, serving in a publicity role for the Art Theatre Guild—an organization founded by her husband Louis K. Sher that operated a national chain of art-house cinemas. In this capacity, she worked closely with Mike Getz, the Underground Film Coordinator, who was a central figure in the distribution of experimental cinema. Her professional activities extended into multimedia design and production; she is cited as an employer and collaborator for artists working in multimedia design and staging techniques, such as the artist Vladimer. This background in theatrical production and multimedia technology aligns with the interdisciplinary goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Later in life, she resided in Manhattan and was married to Frederick Evans.
